question: Explain the primary difference between an array and a linked list. In your answer, identify one key advantage and one key disadvantage of a linked list over an array.
expectedPoints: Arrays provide direct access to elements via an index, while linked lists require sequential traversal from the head node to access an element.
A key advantage of a linked list is its efficient insertion and deletion operations, as they only require updating pointers, unlike arrays which may require shifting elements.
A key disadvantage of a linked list is its higher memory overhead per element due to the storage of pointers/references to the next node.

question: Describe the 'Stack' data structure. Name and describe its two fundamental operations.
expectedPoints: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle.
The push operation adds an element to the top of the stack.
The pop operation removes and returns the element from the top of the stack.

question: Compare and contrast the time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion operations in a Binary Search Tree (BST) versus a balanced BST (like an AVL tree) in both the average and worst-case scenarios. Explain why these differences occur.
expectedPoints: In an average-case BST, search, insertion, and deletion are O(log n) operations, but they degrade to O(n) in the worst-case scenario (e.g., a skewed tree).
A balanced BST (AVL tree) maintains an O(log n) time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion in both the average and worst-case scenarios.

This difference occurs because a balanced BST uses rotation operations during insertion and deletion to maintain a height that is logarithmic to the number of nodes, preventing the tree from becoming a linear chain.
